question_title: Will the Florida Legislature pass the FY 2026-27 General Appropriations Act before May 31, 2026?
summary: The Florida Legislature is highly likely to pass the FY 2026-27 budget before May 31, but not certain. Strong structural factors favor YES: both chambers passed initial versions with overwhelming margins (98-8 and 32-0), the top-line GR framework is already agreed ($51.98B), the remaining gap is only ~1.3% of the total budget, and the political costs of failure (government shutdown) are enormous. The Senate President targets completion the week of May 25. However, meaningful timing risk exists: as of May 21 there was no public progress, Speaker Perez hasn't endorsed the timeline, the 72-hour cooling off period creates a hard filing deadline of ~May 27, and the 2025 precedent shows these negotiations can drag well past initial deadlines. The behind-closed-doors phase is described as normal FL budget process, and the WFLA headline suggests a final vote next week. I estimate 82% probability, reflecting high confidence that a deal will be reached but acknowledging the tight timeline constraint and demonstrated dysfunction in this legislative session.

factors.description: Both chambers passed initial versions with overwhelming bipartisan margins (98-8 House, 32-0 Senate), demonstrating strong legislative will to complete the budget
Special session convened solely for budget completion with pre-agreed $51.98B GR framework - the hard part (topline agreement) is done
Budget gap is only ~1.3% ($1.45B of ~$114B total) - line-item disagreements, not fundamental policy opposition
Failure to pass budget before July 1 would cause government shutdown - enormous political cost for GOP-controlled legislature
Senate President Albritton publicly targets week of May 25 for completion; WFLA headline says 'final vote next week'
Conference subcommittees at Offer #2 stage, Higher Ed near 'close' - typical late-stage endgame dynamics underway
As of May 21 (Day 10), WCTV reports 'no public progress' with negotiations behind closed doors, though this is described as normal FL budget process
House Speaker Perez has NOT endorsed Senate's timeline and pushes for deeper cuts - brinkmanship risk
2025 precedent: similar situation resulted in 45-day extension to June 16, suggesting these disputes can drag well past initial deadlines
72-hour constitutional cooling off period constrains timing: conference report must be filed by May 27 at absolute latest for a vote before midnight May 31
Only ~2 days of buffer between session end (May 29) and question deadline (May 31 midnight) if session needs extension

reasoning:
## Forecast: Will the Florida Legislature pass the FY 2026-27 General Appropriations Act before May 31, 2026?

**Probability: 0.82 (82%)**

### Key Reasoning

**For YES (~82%):**
- Both chambers already passed initial versions with overwhelming support (House 98-8, Senate 32-0 unanimous)
- Top-line spending framework already agreed ($51.98B General Revenue)
- The $1.45B gap is only ~1.3% of the total budget — line-item haggling, not fundamental opposition
- Government shutdown risk creates massive political incentive for the GOP-controlled legislature
- Senate President Albritton publicly targets completion week of May 25
- Conference subcommittees progressing normally (Offer #2 stage, Higher Ed near closure)
- Session can extend ~2 days past May 29 and still resolve YES

**For NO (~18%):**
- As of May 21, WCTV reports "no public progress" after 10 days of session
- Speaker Perez hasn't endorsed the Senate's timeline, pushing for deeper cuts
- 72-hour cooling off period creates a hard filing deadline of ~May 27 (only 5 days away)
- 2025 precedent: similar budget impasse dragged 45 days past the regular session (to June 16)
- Dozens of politically charged line-item gaps remain (Everglades, State Guard, Rays stadium)
- This is the Legislature's unprecedented 5th special session of 2026, reflecting deep dysfunction
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the Florida Legislature pass the FY 2026-27 General Appropriations Act before May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43573)
[Florida budget special session enters day 10 with no public progress](https://www.wctv.tv/2026/05/21/florida-budget-special-session-enters-day-10-with-no-public-progress/)

[Florida special session: House, Senate and DeSantis billions apart on spending plan as budget deadline nears](https://www.fox13news.com/news/florida-special-session-house-senate-desantis-billions-spending-plan-budget-deadline)

[Budget conference: Senate, House make strides on environment, ag, but dozens of gaps remain](https://floridapolitics.com/archives/796823-budget-conference-senate-house-make-strides-on-environment-ag-but-dozens-of-gaps-remain/)
[Behind closed doors: How Florida’s budget gets made](https://www.wusf.org/politics-issues/2026-05-20/behind-closed-doors-how-florida-budget-gets-made)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, before May 31, 2026 (00:00 Eastern Time), both chambers of the Florida Legislature have given final passage to HB 5001-E (the FY 2026-27 General Appropriations Act) or any successor/substitute General Appropriations Act bill filed during the 2026E Special Session, meaning both the Florida House and Florida Senate have adopted the final conference report (or otherwise passed an identical version) and the bill has been enrolled / ordered engrossed for transmittal to the Governor. Resolution will be determined by the bill history shown on the Florida Senate's official page for HB 5001-E (https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026E/5001), where the bill history must reflect final passage in both chambers. If a different bill number is used as the vehicle, the corresponding Florida Senate bill page for that 2026E General Appropriations Act will be used. If the Florida Senate's website is unavailable, the Florida House bill tracking page (https://www.flhouse.gov) will be used as a fallback.
fine_print: "Final passage" means the chamber's adoption of the conference report (or, if no conference is needed, concurrence in the other chamber's version), not the initial passage votes of May 12, 2026. The Governor's signature is NOT required for this question to resolve YES. If the special session is extended past May 29 but final passage still occurs before May 31, 2026 (00:00 ET), the question resolves YES. If final passage occurs at any time on or after 00:00 ET May 31, 2026, the question resolves NO.

factors.description: Both chambers already passed initial versions with overwhelming margins (98-8 House, 32-0 Senate), demonstrating strong will to complete the budget
Special session explicitly convened solely for budget completion - this is the Legislature's only constitutional obligation
Top-line allocations already agreed ($51.98B GR framework) - disagreements are on line items, not overall spending level
Senate President Albritton publicly targets completion week of May 25 - leadership committed to the timeline
Failure to pass budget before July 1 would cause government shutdown - enormous political cost for the Republican-controlled legislature
As of May 21, no public progress reported (Day 10), negotiations behind closed doors, dozens of gaps remain
House Speaker Perez has NOT endorsed Senate's timeline and has been pushing for deeper cuts - potential for brinkmanship
72-hour cooling off period constrains timing - conference report must be filed by May 27 at latest (if session extended)
2025 precedent: FL legislature also failed regular session budget and extended 45 days, but DID eventually pass it (albeit in June)
Session can be extended past May 29 but must still conclude before May 31 midnight for YES - only ~2 extra days of buffer
Conference is moving - subcommittees at Offer #2 stage, Higher Ed near 'close' offer, this is typical late-stage budget dynamics
Budget gap is only ~1.3% of total budget ($1.45B of $114B) - this is bridgeable, not fundamental disagreement

input.anchor: Historical base rate: In Florida's modern history, the legislature has ALWAYS eventually passed a budget (constitutional requirement). The question is about timing. In 2025, they took 45 extra days (session ended June 16). In 2024, budget passed during regular session. The base rate of passing a budget within a dedicated special session window is very high but not 100%, given that the session can be extended and there's only a ~2 day buffer after May 29.
input.assessment:
This question asks whether the Florida legislature will pass its budget before May 31, 2026 midnight ET. The special session runs May 12-29 (18 days). 

KEY FACTORS FOR YES:
1. Both chambers have demonstrated overwhelming support for a budget deal (98-8 and 32-0 initial votes)
2. Top-line allocations already agreed at $51.98B GR
3. The gap is only ~$1.45B on a $114B budget (1.3%) - bridgeable
4. Constitutional requirement and government shutdown risk create massive political incentive
5. Conference committees actively negotiating, with at least one subcommittee near closure
6. Senate President publicly targets week of May 25 for completion
7. The session can be extended 1-2 days beyond May 29 and still resolve YES

KEY FACTORS FOR NO:
1. As of May 21 (Day 10), WCTV reports "no public progress" with negotiations behind closed doors
2. House Speaker Perez hasn't endorsed the Senate's timeline
3. In 2025, the legislature took 45 extra days (until June 16) to pass the budget - suggesting these disputes can drag on
4. Dozens of line-item gaps remain, including politically charged items (Everglades, State Guard, Rays stadium)
5. 72-hour cooling off constrains timing: conference report must be filed by May 27 at latest
6. Memorial Day (May 25) falls during the critical final week

ASSESSMENT: The structure of this situation strongly favors YES. Florida legislatures do not have a pattern of missing self-imposed budget deadlines in special sessions without attempting to extend. The political costs of failure are enormous. The $1.45B gap is not a fundamental disagreement - it's line-item haggling, which is the normal end-game of every budget. The behind-closed-doors negotiation with "no public progress" is actually the normal pattern (WUSF article explicitly says the secrecy isn't abnormal). 

The main risk is the 2025 precedent where it took until June 16. But several factors differ: in 2025, the session was extended within the regular session framework; here, a special session was specifically called for the budget with an agreed GR framework. The infrastructure for a deal is already built.

I estimate ~85% probability of YES.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor: ~80% base rate for passing within a dedicated special session window when framework is agreed. Factor sum pushes higher. The main anchoring risk is that I might be overweighting the political incentives and underweighting the demonstrated dysfunction (this is the 5th special session of 2026). Status quo analysis: if nothing changes from May 21 status (no progress), the budget would NOT pass by May 31 - but "no public progress" may not mean "no actual progress" given behind-closed-doors negotiations.
input.key_uncertainties:
1. Whether the behind-closed-doors negotiations are actually making progress (WCTV says no public progress, but this is normal in FL budget)
2. Whether Speaker Perez is willing to run out the clock for leverage or whether he's genuinely blocking
3. Whether specific hot-button issues (Rays stadium, State Guard, Everglades) are dealbreakers or tradeable chips
4. Whether 72-hour cooling period is strictly enforced or can be waived
input.tool_audit:
Useful: research() provided excellent detailed data on conference committee structure and progress. Web search found critical WCTV article from May 21 showing no public progress. Senate bill page confirmed conference status. 2025 precedent data was very informative.
Empty: Could not find any very recent (May 22) reporting on a deal.
Failures: Florida House website (flhouse.gov) consistently returned access denied. WFLA article returned 403.
input.update_triggers:
1. Conference report filed → probability jumps to ~95%+
2. Public statement from either leader saying deal is imminent → probability rises to ~90%
3. Announcement of session extension beyond May 29 → depends on tone, but suggests difficulty
4. Public breakdown in negotiations or walkout → probability drops to ~40-50%
5. Announcement that budget will move to June → resolves NO
input.process_reflection: The research tool worked well for getting structured data from the Florida Senate website. Web search was effective at finding recent news articles. The main limitation was that the most critical data point - whether a conference agreement has been reached TODAY - is not yet reported on. The 72-hour cooling off timeline calculation was useful to run in code. The question is time-sensitive enough that even a few hours of delay in information could matter significantly.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Resolution criteria**: Present, detailed, and well-engaged. The agent fetched the Metaculus question page and its factors directly address the specific terms (conference report adoption, final passage in both chambers, May 31 midnight ET deadline, Florida Senate bill page as authoritative source). ✓

**Factor verification against trace**: All 12 factors have clear support in the research trace:
- Factor 1 (votes 98-8, 32-0): Confirmed by FL Senate bill page and research results.
- Factor 3 ($51.98B GR framework, $1.45B gap): Confirmed across multiple sources (flsenate.gov, CBS Miami, Tallahassee Democrat).
- Factor 4 (Albritton targets week of May 25): Confirmed by FloridaPolitics article on environment/ag gaps.
- Factor 6 (No public progress Day 10): Confirmed by WCTV article fetched in trace.
- Factor 9 (2025 precedent, 45 extra days, June 16): Confirmed by Florida College Access Network source in search results.
- Factor 11 (Higher Ed near "close" offer): Confirmed by research results showing House "Close" offer filed.
- Factor 12 (gap ~1.3%): $1.45B / ~$114B ≈ 1.27%, confirmed.
- All other factors similarly grounded. No hallucinated evidence found.

**Double-counting**: Factors 1, 2, 3, 5, and 12 share the underlying theme of "legislature is motivated to pass a budget." The agent explicitly recognized this non-independence in the trace ("my factors aren't truly independent") and adjusted the factor-implied 99.9% down to 85%. This is responsible handling.

**Wrong-direction factors**: All logit signs correct. Positive factors genuinely favor YES; negative factors genuinely favor NO.

**Cross-question consistency**: The agent previously forecast Q43550 ("Will session adjourn sine die by May 29?") at 70% and Q43545 (numeric budget amount) with ~15% no-deal probability. The current 85% YES for "pass before May 31" is arithmetically consistent: P(pass by May 31) = P(pass by May 29) + P(pass May 30 | not done by May 29) × P(not done by May 29) ≈ 70% + 50% × 30% = 85%. The extra ~2 days of buffer justify the 15pp premium. The numeric forecast's built-in 15% no-deal assumption also matches perfectly.

**Counterargument quality**: Strong. The agent constructs a coherent alternative story citing the 2025 precedent, Day 10 lack of progress (WCTV), Speaker Perez's non-endorsement, the 72-hour constraint creating a hard May 27 filing deadline, and specific politically charged line items. It also specifies concrete what-would-change-my-mind triggers with probability shifts. Not a token gesture. ✓

**Confidence assessment**: 0.50 is appropriately modest given genuine timing uncertainty. ✓

**Probability assessment**: 85% is well-calibrated for this evidence base. The structural incentives strongly favor passage (constitutional obligation, small gap relative to total budget, overwhelming initial votes, agreed GR framework). The main risk is the tight timeline with the 72-hour cooling off period constraining the effective deadline to ~May 27. The 2025 precedent of 45-day delay is the most compelling counter-evidence, but the agent correctly notes the different structural setup (dedicated special session with pre-agreed framework vs. regular session breakdown). My independent estimate would be 80-87%, placing the agent's 85% squarely within range.

**Tool audit**: Agent claims flhouse.gov "consistently returned access denied" - the rejections appear in web search snippets rather than dedicated fetch_url calls. The WFLA 403 is confirmed. Minor exaggeration but immaterial to the forecast.

No errors warranting a warn or fail were identified.
